facts.proposedValue: This repository implements a scalable Bayesian hierarchical model for daily store-level sales forecasting, accompanying the blog post "Finally! Bayesian Hierarchical Modelling at Scale". It uses JAX and NumPyro to fit a Gamma-Poisson model that pools information across many retail stores while retaining store-specific effects. Data is downloaded from the Rossmann store sales Kaggle dataset, preprocessed into a feature cube, and trained using stochastic variational inference. The notebooks demonstrate end-to-end preprocessing, model fitting, posterior analysis, and comparison against a conventional linear regression baseline. The project is intended as reproducible, research-grade code rather than a production deployment.

proposedValue: Using the model’s forecasts directly for safety-critical or legally regulated decisions (e.g., healthcare, credit, or public safety) without additional domain validation and governance.
Applying the example code to sensitive or personal data sets without adding appropriate privacy, security, and compliance controls.
facts.verifierNotes: Neither the LICENSE excerpt nor the README snippet includes any warnings or statements about hazardous or safety‑critical use, sensitive data, or compliance/privacy constraints. The proposed hazardous use cases are not grounded in the provided text.

facts.proposedValue: The intended use of this repository is to serve as a reproducible, educational example of Bayesian hierarchical modelling at scale, showing how to implement a Gamma-Poisson sales forecasting model with JAX/NumPyro on the Rossmann store sales dataset. It is meant for experimentation in notebooks, methodological exploration, and as a template for adapting similar probabilistic models to other tabular time-series problems.

proposedValue: Providing a production-ready forecasting service or managed API; the code is organised as notebooks and a research-focused Python package rather than a deployed application.
Building a generic, fully parameterised forecasting library; the implementation is tailored to the Rossmann store sales example used in the accompanying blog post.
Real-time, low-latency prediction serving; the project is designed around offline batch inference in notebooks and file-based I/O.
Turnkey support for arbitrary datasets; adapting the code to new domains requires manual preprocessing and model adjustments.

facts.proposedValue: Forecasting daily sales for many retail stores using a Bayesian hierarchical Gamma-Poisson model that shares statistical strength across stores while capturing store-specific effects, demonstrated on the Rossmann store sales Kaggle dataset.

proposedValue: Data scientists and statisticians interested in Bayesian hierarchical models for time-series and count data.
Machine learning researchers and engineers experimenting with scalable probabilistic programming using JAX and NumPyro.
Download Rossmann store sales data (train.csv and store.csv) from Kaggle into data/raw using kaggle.api.dataset_download_files in notebooks/01-preprocessing.ipynb.

Clean and feature-engineer the joined sales and store tables with pandas (handling holidays, assortment, store type, and time indices) and write the intermediate DataFrame df and encoded edf.csv to data/preprocessed and data/result.

Dummy-encode categorical variables (e.g., DayOfWeek, StateHoliday, StoreVariant) and reshape the data into a StoreId × Timestep × (features + target) cube via bhm_at_scale.preprocess.make_cube, saving X_train.npz and X_test.npz.

Load X_train in notebooks/02-model.ipynb, initialise the Gamma-Poisson hierarchical model and guide from bhm_at_scale.model, and train parameters with ModelHandler.fit using NumPyro’s SVI and an Adam optimizer.

Use ModelHandler.predict and predictive_model to generate posterior predictive sales trajectories for train and test stores, save summary CSVs (e.g., train_preds.csv, test_preds.csv, stats.csv) under data/result, and visualise store-level histories and global weekday/promotion effects in notebooks/03-evaluation.ipynb via PlotStore and plot_densities.
facts.verifierNotes: The provided notebook snippets only show imports and general setup; they do not contain any explicit code for Kaggle downloads, pandas cleaning/feature engineering, dummy-encoding, make_cube usage, saving X_train/X_test, model training with ModelHandler.fit, or prediction/plotting in the described data flow. Therefore, the detailed multi-step pipeline cannot be verified from the given evidence.

proposedValue: Core scientific Python stack: numpy, scipy, pandas, scikit-learn, matplotlib, seaborn (defined in environment.yaml).
Probabilistic programming and JAX ecosystem: jax, jaxlib, numpyro for hierarchical modelling and stochastic variational inference (from environment.lock.yaml and notebooks/02-model.ipynb).
Supporting libraries and tooling: kaggle API client for data download, daft for graphical model plotting, jupyterlab for notebook-driven experimentation.
facts.verifierNotes: The environment.yaml and environment.lock.yaml snippets do confirm parts of the claim: presence of numpy, scipy, pandas, scikit-learn, matplotlib, seaborn, kaggle, numpyro, daft, and jupyterlab. However, the statement also asserts jaxlib and specific usage of these libraries for hierarchical modelling and SVI, and describes kaggle as an API client for data download. The snippets do not list jaxlib, do not show actual use of jax/numpyro for hierarchical modelling in the notebook, and do not show use of the Kaggle API. Under the strict-evidence rule, the full described dependency usage cannot be validated.

handler.py shows the ModelHandler class and its constructor, but the snippet is truncated before any fit or predict methods; their existence and signatures are not visible. model.py shows type aliases and helper classes but does not include the model or local_guide functions in the visible portion. preprocess.make_cube is not shown at all. plot.py shows a function plot_sales_preds, but there is no visible PlotStore API. Thus the listed public APIs (ModelHandler.fit, ModelHandler.predict, bhm_at_scale.model.model, bhm_at_scale.model.local_guide, bhm_at_scale.preprocess.make_cube, bhm_at_scale.plot.PlotStore) are not all explicitly supported by the provided code segments.

proposedValue.name: Data preprocessing and feature cube construction
proposedValue.summary: Loads Rossmann store and sales CSVs from Kaggle, performs cleansing and basic feature engineering with pandas, dummy-encodes categorical variables, and reshapes the data into a StoreId × Timestep feature cube suitable for hierarchical modelling.

proposedValue.name: Gamma-Poisson hierarchical sales model
proposedValue.summary: Defines a Gamma-Poisson hierarchical model for daily store sales and its variational guides using NumPyro primitives, including global and per-store coefficients and dispersion parameters, plus helpers for predictive inference and local adaptation to new stores.

proposedValue.name: Model training and SVI handler
proposedValue.summary: Wraps NumPyro’s SVI API in a ModelHandler class that manages optimizer state, training loops, logging, checkpointing of optimizer state, and convenient prediction via Predictive for arbitrary input tensors.

proposedValue.name: Evaluation and visualization
proposedValue.summary: Reads posterior summaries and predictions, visualises store-level sales histories with promotion and holiday overlays, and plots global weekday and promotion effects to compare hierarchical estimates with per-store coefficients.

facts.proposedValue: pytest is configured to run tests with coverage on the bhm_at_scale package (via setup.cfg [tool:pytest] and .coveragerc), but no coverage reports are checked into the repository.
facts.verifierNotes: .coveragerc and setup.cfg show that coverage is configured for the bhm_at_scale package via pytest, but there is no evidence in the snippets about whether coverage report files are or are not checked into the repository.

proposedValue: Source code is licensed under GPL-2.0-or-later (GNU General Public License version 2), so redistribution and use must comply with the GPL terms.
Conventional linear regression on log-transformed sales using scikit-learn’s LinearRegression as a Poisson-style baseline for a single store.

patch.value: This repository implements a scalable Bayesian hierarchical model for daily store-level sales forecasting, accompanying the blog post "Finally! Bayesian Hierarchical Modelling at Scale". It uses JAX and NumPyro to fit a Gamma-Poisson model that pools information across many retail stores while retaining store-specific effects. Data is downloaded from the Rossmann store sales Kaggle dataset, preprocessed into a feature cube, and trained using stochastic variational inference. The notebooks demonstrate end-to-end preprocessing, model fitting, posterior analysis, and comparison against a conventional linear regression baseline. The project is intended as reproducible, research-grade code rather than a production deployment.

The intended use of this repository is to serve as a reproducible, educational example of Bayesian hierarchical modelling at scale, showing how to implement a Gamma-Poisson sales forecasting model with JAX/NumPyro on the Rossmann store sales dataset. It is meant for experimentation in notebooks, methodological exploration, and as a template for adapting similar probabilistic models to other tabular time-series problems.

Forecasting daily sales for many retail stores using a Bayesian hierarchical Gamma-Poisson model that shares statistical strength across stores while capturing store-specific effects, demonstrated on the Rossmann store sales Kaggle dataset.
Providing a production-ready forecasting service or managed API; the code is organised as notebooks and a research-focused Python package rather than a deployed application.
Building a generic, fully parameterised forecasting library; the implementation is tailored to the Rossmann store sales example used in the accompanying blog post.
Real-time, low-latency prediction serving; the project is designed around offline batch inference in notebooks and file-based I/O.
Turnkey support for arbitrary datasets; adapting the code to new domains requires manual preprocessing and model adjustments.
Data scientists and statisticians interested in Bayesian hierarchical models for time-series and count data.
Machine learning researchers and engineers experimenting with scalable probabilistic programming using JAX and NumPyro.
Using the model’s forecasts directly for safety-critical or legally regulated decisions (e.g., healthcare, credit, or public safety) without additional domain validation and governance.
Applying the example code to sensitive or personal data sets without adding appropriate privacy, security, and compliance controls.

value.name: Data preprocessing and feature cube construction
value.summary: Loads Rossmann store and sales CSVs from Kaggle, performs cleansing and basic feature engineering with pandas, dummy-encodes categorical variables, and reshapes the data into a StoreId × Timestep feature cube suitable for hierarchical modelling.

value.name: Gamma-Poisson hierarchical sales model
value.summary: Defines a Gamma-Poisson hierarchical model for daily store sales and its variational guides using NumPyro primitives, including global and per-store coefficients and dispersion parameters, plus helpers for predictive inference and local adaptation to new stores.

value.name: Model training and SVI handler
value.summary: Wraps NumPyro’s SVI API in a ModelHandler class that manages optimizer state, training loops, logging, checkpointing of optimizer state, and convenient prediction via Predictive for arbitrary input tensors.

value.name: Evaluation and visualization
value.summary: Reads posterior summaries and predictions, visualises store-level sales histories with promotion and holiday overlays, and plots global weekday and promotion effects to compare hierarchical estimates with per-store coefficients.

patch.value: Download Rossmann store sales data (train.csv and store.csv) from Kaggle into data/raw using kaggle.api.dataset_download_files in notebooks/01-preprocessing.ipynb.

Clean and feature-engineer the joined sales and store tables with pandas (handling holidays, assortment, store type, and time indices) and write the intermediate DataFrame df and encoded edf.csv to data/preprocessed and data/result.

Dummy-encode categorical variables (e.g., DayOfWeek, StateHoliday, StoreVariant) and reshape the data into a StoreId × Timestep × (features + target) cube via bhm_at_scale.preprocess.make_cube, saving X_train.npz and X_test.npz.

Load X_train in notebooks/02-model.ipynb, initialise the Gamma-Poisson hierarchical model and guide from bhm_at_scale.model, and train parameters with ModelHandler.fit using NumPyro’s SVI and an Adam optimizer.

Use ModelHandler.predict and predictive_model to generate posterior predictive sales trajectories for train and test stores, save summary CSVs (e.g., train_preds.csv, test_preds.csv, stats.csv) under data/result, and visualise store-level histories and global weekday/promotion effects in notebooks/03-evaluation.ipynb via PlotStore and plot_densities.
Core scientific Python stack: numpy, scipy, pandas, scikit-learn, matplotlib, seaborn (defined in environment.yaml).
Probabilistic programming and JAX ecosystem: jax, jaxlib, numpyro for hierarchical modelling and stochastic variational inference (from environment.lock.yaml and notebooks/02-model.ipynb).
Supporting libraries and tooling: kaggle API client for data download, daft for graphical model plotting, jupyterlab for notebook-driven experimentation.
pytest is configured to run tests with coverage on the bhm_at_scale package (via setup.cfg [tool:pytest] and .coveragerc), but no coverage reports are checked into the repository.

card_patch.value: This repository implements a scalable Bayesian hierarchical model for daily store-level sales forecasting, accompanying the blog post "Finally! Bayesian Hierarchical Modelling at Scale". It uses JAX and NumPyro to fit a Gamma-Poisson model that pools information across many retail stores while retaining store-specific effects. Data is downloaded from the Rossmann store sales Kaggle dataset, preprocessed into a feature cube, and trained using stochastic variational inference. The notebooks demonstrate end-to-end preprocessing, model fitting, posterior analysis, and comparison against a conventional linear regression baseline. The project is intended as reproducible, research-grade code rather than a production deployment.

The intended use of this repository is to serve as a reproducible, educational example of Bayesian hierarchical modelling at scale, showing how to implement a Gamma-Poisson sales forecasting model with JAX/NumPyro on the Rossmann store sales dataset. It is meant for experimentation in notebooks, methodological exploration, and as a template for adapting similar probabilistic models to other tabular time-series problems.

Forecasting daily sales for many retail stores using a Bayesian hierarchical Gamma-Poisson model that shares statistical strength across stores while capturing store-specific effects, demonstrated on the Rossmann store sales Kaggle dataset.
Providing a production-ready forecasting service or managed API; the code is organised as notebooks and a research-focused Python package rather than a deployed application.
Building a generic, fully parameterised forecasting library; the implementation is tailored to the Rossmann store sales example used in the accompanying blog post.
Real-time, low-latency prediction serving; the project is designed around offline batch inference in notebooks and file-based I/O.
Turnkey support for arbitrary datasets; adapting the code to new domains requires manual preprocessing and model adjustments.
Data scientists and statisticians interested in Bayesian hierarchical models for time-series and count data.
Machine learning researchers and engineers experimenting with scalable probabilistic programming using JAX and NumPyro.
Using the model’s forecasts directly for safety-critical or legally regulated decisions (e.g., healthcare, credit, or public safety) without additional domain validation and governance.
Applying the example code to sensitive or personal data sets without adding appropriate privacy, security, and compliance controls.

value.name: Data preprocessing and feature cube construction
value.summary: Loads Rossmann store and sales CSVs from Kaggle, performs cleansing and basic feature engineering with pandas, dummy-encodes categorical variables, and reshapes the data into a StoreId × Timestep feature cube suitable for hierarchical modelling.

value.name: Gamma-Poisson hierarchical sales model
value.summary: Defines a Gamma-Poisson hierarchical model for daily store sales and its variational guides using NumPyro primitives, including global and per-store coefficients and dispersion parameters, plus helpers for predictive inference and local adaptation to new stores.

value.name: Model training and SVI handler
value.summary: Wraps NumPyro’s SVI API in a ModelHandler class that manages optimizer state, training loops, logging, checkpointing of optimizer state, and convenient prediction via Predictive for arbitrary input tensors.

value.name: Evaluation and visualization
value.summary: Reads posterior summaries and predictions, visualises store-level sales histories with promotion and holiday overlays, and plots global weekday and promotion effects to compare hierarchical estimates with per-store coefficients.

card_patch.value: Download Rossmann store sales data (train.csv and store.csv) from Kaggle into data/raw using kaggle.api.dataset_download_files in notebooks/01-preprocessing.ipynb.

Clean and feature-engineer the joined sales and store tables with pandas (handling holidays, assortment, store type, and time indices) and write the intermediate DataFrame df and encoded edf.csv to data/preprocessed and data/result.

Dummy-encode categorical variables (e.g., DayOfWeek, StateHoliday, StoreVariant) and reshape the data into a StoreId × Timestep × (features + target) cube via bhm_at_scale.preprocess.make_cube, saving X_train.npz and X_test.npz.

Load X_train in notebooks/02-model.ipynb, initialise the Gamma-Poisson hierarchical model and guide from bhm_at_scale.model, and train parameters with ModelHandler.fit using NumPyro’s SVI and an Adam optimizer.

Use ModelHandler.predict and predictive_model to generate posterior predictive sales trajectories for train and test stores, save summary CSVs (e.g., train_preds.csv, test_preds.csv, stats.csv) under data/result, and visualise store-level histories and global weekday/promotion effects in notebooks/03-evaluation.ipynb via PlotStore and plot_densities.
Core scientific Python stack: numpy, scipy, pandas, scikit-learn, matplotlib, seaborn (defined in environment.yaml).
Probabilistic programming and JAX ecosystem: jax, jaxlib, numpyro for hierarchical modelling and stochastic variational inference (from environment.lock.yaml and notebooks/02-model.ipynb).
Supporting libraries and tooling: kaggle API client for data download, daft for graphical model plotting, jupyterlab for notebook-driven experimentation.
pytest is configured to run tests with coverage on the bhm_at_scale package (via setup.cfg [tool:pytest] and .coveragerc), but no coverage reports are checked into the repository.

hyperparams.batching: full-batch over all stores and days

value.hardware: Not specified (depends on available JAX device)

card_patch.value: Gamma-Poisson hierarchical regression for daily store-level sales forecasting on count data, modelling shared global effects and store-specific coefficients over time.

value.name: Rossmann Store Sales (Kaggle)
value.license: Kaggle dataset subject to Kaggle terms of use

card_patch.value: Conventional linear regression on log-transformed sales using scikit-learn’s LinearRegression as a Poisson-style baseline for a single store.
Source code is licensed under GPL-2.0-or-later (GNU General Public License version 2), so redistribution and use must comply with the GPL terms.
